Title: Interaction of γ-Fe2O3 nanoparticles with Citrus maxima leaves and the corresponding physiological effects via foliar application

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Natural resistance-associated macrophage protein (Nramp) family metal transporter
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential micronutrient for plant growth and physiological reactions
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Upregulated by iron starvation according to the paper
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Measured in leaves; downregulated under 20–100 mg/L γ-Fe2O3 NP exposure; higher under 100 mg/L Fe3+ and Fe(II)-EDTA
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Downregulation under γ-Fe2O3 NP exposure is associated with iron-sufficient status in leaves
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-PCR gene expression analysis; foliar treatments with γ-Fe2O3 nanoparticles, Fe3+, and Fe(II)-EDTA; shoot/root iron quantification by atomic absorption
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Nramp3 expression was significantly lower than control under 20–100 mg/L γ-Fe2O3 NP treatments. 2. The paper states Nramp3 transports Fe2+ and is upregulated by iron starvation. 3. Reduced Nramp3 expression under NP exposure was interpreted as evidence that plants were in iron-sufficient status.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Foliar spray with 20, 50, 100 mg/L γ-Fe2O3 nanoparticles or matched Fe3+; comparison with Fe-deficient control and 50 μM Fe(II)-EDTA
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Higher Nramp3 expression under 100 mg/L Fe3+ and Fe(II)-EDTA suggested these treatments may not alleviate iron deficiency effectively via foliar spray
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential marker gene for monitoring iron sufficiency status during foliar iron fertilization
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper explicitly notes citrus IRT1 had not been sequenced, so Nramp3 and FRO2 were used as iron-related markers.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Protective barrier on leaf epidermis involved in defense and particle incorporation/barrier properties
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Measured in leaves; lower or unchanged under γ-Fe2O3 NP and Fe3+ treatments; much higher under Fe(II)-EDTA
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ABCG12 expression was lower or unchanged despite increased wax content under some treatments, indicating wax transport is multigenic and complex
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-PCR gene expression analysis; wax load measurement by chloroform extraction and gravimetric quantification
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ABCG12 expression in leaves was lower or unchanged under γ-Fe2O3 NP and Fe3+ treatments compared with control. 2. Wax content still increased under 100 mg/L γ-Fe2O3 NPs and 20–100 mg/L Fe3+. 3. The paper concludes wax secretion/transport is controlled by multiple genes rather than ABCG12 alone.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Foliar spray with γ-Fe2O3 nanoparticles, Fe3+, or Fe(II)-EDTA
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Higher wax may hinder uptake of high levels of nanoparticles or Fe3+
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate marker for studying wax transport responses to foliar nutrient formulations, but insufficient alone to predict wax accumulation
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included in Results as a wax-related core measured gene, but not shown to be singly causal for wax increase.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces ferric iron (Fe3+) to ferrous iron (Fe2+) for plant utilization
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential micronutrient supporting plant physiological reactions
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies usable iron by converting ferric to ferrous form
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established as rate-limiting; supported as an iron-deficiency responsive indicator and key iron activation step
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Activated when plants lack available iron
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High in untreated iron-deficient control leaves; downregulated under γ-Fe2O3 NP, Fe3+, and Fe(II)-EDTA treatments
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Lower FRO2 expression under foliar iron treatments indicates plants were in iron-replete/sufficient status and could utilize supplied iron
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-PCR expression analysis in leaves; foliar treatments; shoot/root iron quantification
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FRO2 expression was high in untreated control plants. 2. γ-Fe2O3 NP and Fe3+ treatments decreased FRO2 expression by 29.4–91.4% versus control. 3. Downregulation was interpreted as evidence that foliar-applied iron from nanoparticles or ions was utilizable and induced iron sufficiency.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Foliar spray with 20–100 mg/L γ-Fe2O3 nanoparticles or Fe3+; comparison with Fe-deficient control and 50 μM Fe(II)-EDTA
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: None directly reported for FRO2 itself; high Fe3+ treatments associated with oxidative stress in shoots
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful molecular marker to assess effectiveness of foliar iron fertilizers and iron sufficiency status
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper discusses leaf mesophyll ferric reductase activity and uses FRO2 as evidence for foliar iron utilization.

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Wax inducer transcriptional regulator; AP2/ERF-type wax-associated regulator (inferred from gene identity)
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Leaf epidermal protective barrier contributing to stress resistance and influencing nanoparticle interaction/incorporation
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional regulation of wax formation-associated genes
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Wax biosynthesis pathway genes; specific direct targets not experimentally validated in this paper
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Foliar exposure to γ-Fe2O3 nanoparticles, Fe3+, and Fe(II)-EDTA altered expression
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent regulator, but not sufficient alone to explain wax accumulation changes in this study
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Measured in leaves; significantly lower than control under all γ-Fe2O3 NP and Fe3+ treatments
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: WIN1 was downregulated under foliar Fe treatments even when wax content increased, showing wax biosynthesis is collaboratively controlled by multiple genes
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-PCR gene expression analysis; wax quantification by chloroform extraction and gravimetry
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. WIN1 expression was significantly reduced under all γ-Fe2O3 NP and Fe3+ treatments. 2. Despite this, wax content increased under 100 mg/L γ-Fe2O3 NPs and 20–100 mg/L Fe3+. 3. The study concludes wax biosynthesis is a collaborative multigene process and WIN1 alone does not determine final wax load under these conditions.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Foliar spray with γ-Fe2O3 nanoparticles, Fe3+, or Fe(II)-EDTA
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Higher wax accumulation may hinder uptake of high levels of nanoparticles or Fe3+
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for dissecting foliar barrier traits, but should be combined with other wax genes in engineering strategies
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper treats WIN1 as a wax formation-associated gene and emphasizes that increased wax under stress can occur without WIN1 upregulation.
